Welcome back. The next step in the acquisition process is getting a meeting with an owner of the business that you're trying to buy. Now if you've done all your homework and you've been working on your own and you found a non-listed business, you're going to have to take the next step to reach out to that business owner. In other words, trying to see if you can buy their business even though it hasn't been listed for sale. Then you'll have to reach out to the owners cold and on your own. If you're getting the meeting through a business broker, then you'll have to demonstrate to the broker that you're a serious and good candidate for the seller to meet with. Likely, the seller will use their own broker as a buffer and they're going to want to know that you're worth meeting with. You should get the broker to trust you by talking with them and demonstrating your credibility, industry expertise and the fact that you have a deal team and your vision for this type of business that you're looking to acquire and what you can do with it. Do not tell the broker that you'd ideally like to make the deal with little to no money out of your pocket as you may scare them off. It's too early in the process to signal that and you could spook away a great deal by doing so. The goal of meeting with the owners to build a relationship and to filter the information about their business to see if it fits your shortlist with your criteria. Also, you'll be using this meeting to establish your credibility with the seller and as you start to build a relationship with the owner of the business, if you end up doing the deal, there's trust and alignment between the two of you. Trust me, you'll need their help. You're also going to be able to get a good in-person read on whether the seller is serious about selling or just taking the temperature check in their market. What type of owner he or she is, the type of business operator they are and you'll get a gut feeling for whether or not you believe there's transparency and integrity in the transaction. Trust me, I've been in plenty of situations where people listed their business for sale only to see how much value they could get with no serious intention to sell it. Equally, I've worked with off-market deals where the business owners just wanted to know what you might pay them out of curiosity. So you really got to understand keeping that funnel wide is critically important. One more thing you'll want to learn in your in-person meeting is whether or not the business has assets that could potentially be leveraged in a buyout like we discussed in earlier modules. And you'll see how the owner sees their business. Depending on how experienced they are and their temperament for the business, you and the seller could be way off on how you'll eventually value the business, which will definitely waste your time in the negotiation process. You should be looking for signs from how the owner talks about his or her goals in the business. Business founders or business owners see their businesses as their baby, another kid they birthed. And they obviously think it's a great business unless they're in distress. So if they're enthusiastic about their business, about their heritage, about their brand, and they're talking about it, that's not a red flag, but it is important for you to notate it because if they think you're going to try to change the name or do something else, it may offend them and they won't want to move forward with you. But if they're making unreasonable claims about how great their business is or how high they think it can go and value, and you're making the notes on this and they're getting to a place where they're trying to convince you it's worth so much more than where it is, you probably need to walk away because a lot of business owners have their heads in the cloud. I've dealt with thousands of them, trust me. Because you're ultimately looking for a win-win deal, you're not looking to get a better deal than the seller. But in turn, you need to make sure they're not trying to get a better deal of view. Unfortunately, many business owners view their businesses as the best business out there. And they are only interested in getting top dollar. They may think they can get more than what the business is actually worth, and in most cases, in most cases, they will. Don't let that offend you. Remember, these people have built this company from nothing. It's their baby. It's their life. It's their income. Treat it like such. This can sometimes cause people, too, to have unrealistic expectations. The emotional separation in their business can also cause problems. If that's the case, then the quicker you can evaluate that to understand if you can reach a fair deal or not will save you an enormous amount of time and money. Nothing worse than spending thousands of dollars with legal advisors and accountants only to know that you're chasing a bad deal. I'm not recommending you get into negotiations there or talking explicitly about the price. But you need to get a sense of whether the business owner you're talking to that you're sitting across from is reasonable or not. If you get the sense that they'll be too hard to work with, then you're better off moving on and finding a better fit for yourself. It's important to understand, though. They're probably evaluating you the same way. There is one important thing to remember at this stage. Do not, during this meeting, make any mention that you're trying to do a deal with little or no money out of your pocket. Don't play that card. That is not the place for it. Instead, focus on the business and ask questions about its operation, its team, its customer base, the biggest challenges it confronts, things that will give you information into not only how the business owner thinks but how they feel about the business. Also, spend time learning the owner's goals, why he started the business in the first place. You're trying to find out his legacy because a good win-win deal involves you preserving and building upon the previous owner's legacy. Ask the business owner who he or she feels personally responsible for. Tell them about the commitments that they've made to other people and the hopes and dreams for the business after they exit. Play back what the owner shares with you so you can confirm you're hearing them correctly and they feel you're listening. Listen far more than you speak during these meetings. This will go a long way in building a relationship with the business owner. Don't approach this meeting or the acquisition as you're trying to get what you want right there. You're not there to get a better deal than the seller. Look at your job as helping them solidify their legacy, ensuring the businesses in good hands and that the people who worked in the business will continue to have an opportunity to perform and succeed in that business. People that sell their businesses want to know that people that worked with them are taking care of. You need to walk a fine line of telling business owners that for the people who contribute, you will always take care of them. All while helping the owner reap the financial reward they've worked so hard for and many cases actually deserve. By helping them solidify their legacy, you'll be building your own legacy. Now I'm not saying not to build yourself up or signal your importance in the relationship. You obviously are an important part of the owner's future and they need to respect and recognize that as part of a healthy business relationship they're going to need you.
